business_goals: Provide an Event framework to be used by an application
Handle and process events through component interaction
Allow application components to utilize the framework services
technical_constraints: Must handle system termination via STOP handler
Must maintain system in idle state until input is received

phase_1_discovery.initial_documentation_status: Fully developed architecture with class diagrams, component descriptions, and defined component interactions.
evaluator_inquiry_questions: Can the components of the architecture be reused for future projects?
Can the framework be expanded in the future to accommodate a new application or new components?
Will the system handle any input given by the user and handle invalid input?
Is the architecture consistent in behavior?
Can any new application-specific functionality be added to the architecture?
Can the system be modified in short time and cost-effective manner?
Does the architecture perform its event-processing task properly?

hardware_view_description: System accepts input via Command Line Interface (CLI)
Single processor execution implied (sequential event processing)
runtime_constraints: Events must be dispatched in a First-Come-First-Served (FIFO) pattern
System must maintain an 'idle wait' state until input is received
Invalid inputs must be handled gracefully

quantitative_model_parameters.performance_proxy: Number of components involved in a transaction
quantitative_model_parameters.transaction_speed: Qualitative (Fastest possible based on minimal component interaction)

questions: Does the system allow unauthorized access?
Does the architecture portray data confidentiality?
Does the architecture process any task in the fastest possible speed?

raw_candidate_scenarios.description: No unauthorized access to the system
All operations are processed in fastest possible speed
Any failure should be followed by immediate recovery
User errors in using system should be handled
New demands on system functionality should be accommodated
Major parts of the framework should be re-usable for future architectures
Framework modification is cost-effective, fast and in minimal time
Components in framework interact in a coordinated manner
Framework expansion is possible to allow for more sophisticated options
Implementation allows for execution in different environments
Proper data encapsulation and secure data structures
Flexible implementation using other programming languages
Overall consistent behavior expected from the architecture
Framework should be complete, clear and perform exactly as required
Hook up multiple applications to the framework at same time
Change interface from command-driven to window-based

architectural_approaches.description: The system utilizes an event-based model where a central dispatcher or loop manages a FIFO queue of events, triggering specific handlers in components upon receipt of input stimuli.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Directly enables the core business goal of handling and processing events through coordinated component interaction.
By decoupling the event source from the event consumer, new handlers or components can be added to the framework with minimal impact on existing code.

architectural_approaches.description: The framework provides the main execution thread and 'idle wait' state, calling into application-specific components only when registered events occur. This 'Hollywood Principle' (Don't call us, we'll call you) allows components to be passive until needed.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Allows the framework core to be reused across multiple different applications by simply swapping the application-specific components.
Supports the system's ability to accommodate new demands on functionality by plugging in different behaviors into the framework's lifecycle.

architectural_approaches.description: The architecture distinguishes between the Infrastructure/Framework layer (handling CLI, FIFO queuing, and dispatching) and the Application layer (handling specific business logic for events).

addressed_attributes.rationale: Maintains a consistent behavior across the system by isolating event management logic from application-specific logic.
Enables the modification of the user interface (e.g., from CLI to Window-based) within the lower layers without requiring changes to the application logic layer.

children.refinement: Adding new event types without core changes
children.scenario_description: A developer adds a new event type and a corresponding handler to the system; the central dispatcher and existing handlers require no source code modifications.

children.refinement: Swapping CLI for GUI using the layered framework
children.scenario_description: The organization decides to replace the command-line interface with a graphical user interface; the application logic layer remains untouched while only the infrastructure layer is replaced.
The system maintains less than 50ms latency for event processing under a sustained load of 100 events per second.

scenarios.scenario_text: A developer adds a new event type and a corresponding handler to the system; the central dispatcher and existing handlers require no source code modifications.
The organization decides to replace the command-line interface with a graphical user interface; the application logic layer remains untouched while only the infrastructure layer is replaced.
The system maintains less than 50ms latency for event processing under a sustained load of 100 events per second.

scenario_reference.text: A developer adds a new event type and a corresponding handler to the system; the central dispatcher and existing handlers require no source code modifications.

analysis_records.stimulus: Addition of a new event type and handler
analysis_records.response_measure: Zero changes to the central dispatcher or existing handlers
analysis_records.reasoning: The use of an IoC container or a dynamic registration pattern allows handlers to subscribe to the event dispatcher at runtime or through external configuration, removing the need for hardcoded switch statements in the core.

non_risks.rationale: The FIFO queue and central dispatcher ensure that producers of events have no dependency on the specific handlers, facilitating easy extension.

scenario_reference.text: The organization decides to replace the command-line interface with a graphical user interface; the application logic layer remains untouched while only the infrastructure layer is replaced.

analysis_records.reasoning: Strict separation between the Infrastructure (UI) and Application logic ensures that the core logic is not coupled to the specific I/O implementation.

risks.rationale: If the events in the FIFO queue contain UI-specific objects (e.g., CLI string buffers), the application logic will become dependent on the UI layer, breaking reusability.

non_risks.rationale: The framework's enforcement of layer boundaries prevents application logic from calling UI-specific APIs.

sensitivity_points.parameter: Abstraction level of the Event Object

scenario_reference.text: The system maintains less than 50ms latency for event processing under a sustained load of 100 events per second.

analysis_records.reasoning: A single-threaded event loop minimizes context switching costs but processes events sequentially. At 100 events/sec, each event has a 10ms budget to maintain the 50ms threshold including queue wait time.

risks.rationale: In a single-threaded dispatcher, a single long-running handler will block the entire queue, causing latency to spike for all subsequent events.
